name: Lake of the Hurones from El Bosque
name_original: Lago de los Hurones desde El Bosque
region_intro: Road cycling around El Bosque features routes through varied terrain, characterized by significant elevation changes and mountain passes. The region is known for its challenging ascents and descents, with many routes traversing natural landscapes. Cyclists can expect winding roads and scenic views across the area's diverse topography. Best road cycling routes around El Bosque The most popular road cycling route is Lake of the Hurones from El Bosque, a 45.6 miles (73.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 50 minutes to complete. This route features significant climbing and views of the Lake of the Hurones. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Puerto del Mojón de la Víbora – Puerto de Montejaque loop from El Bosque, a difficult 68.6 miles (110.4 km) path. This route includes multiple mountain passes, offering sustained climbs and panoramic vistas. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto de las Palomas – El Boyar Pass loop from El Bosque, a 18.6 miles (30.0 km) trail leading through mountain passes, often completed in about 1 hour 56 minutes. Road cycling around El Bosque is defined by challenging mountain passes, significant elevation gains, and scenic natural landscapes. The network offers options primarily for moderate to advanced cyclists. The routes in El Bosque are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 18 reviews. More than 1,400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore El Bosque's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around El Bosque?
A: There are over 15 road cycling routes around El Bosque, offering a variety of experiences for cyclists. The region is known for its challenging ascents and descents through natural landscapes.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in El Bosque?
A: Road cycling around El Bosque is primarily suited for moderate to advanced cyclists. The routes feature significant elevation changes and mountain passes, with no easy routes currently listed in the area.

Q: What is the typical duration for road cycling routes in El Bosque?
A: Route durations vary significantly depending on the distance and elevation. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e971872028/lake-of-the-hurones-from-el-bosque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lake of the Hurones from El Bosque&lt;/a&gt; route typically takes around 3 hours 50 minutes, while the longer &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5287361" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Mojón de la Víbora – Puerto de Montejaque loop from El Bosque&lt;/a&gt; can take over 5 hours 45 minutes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in El Bosque?
A: El Bosque's road cycling routes are characterized by varied terrain, including challenging mountain passes, significant elevation gains, and winding roads through scenic natural landscapes. You'll encounter sustained climbs and panoramic vistas.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in El Bosque?
A: The komoot community highly rates the road cycling experience in El Bosque, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 18 reviews. Cyclists often praise the challenging mountain passes, the scenic natural beauty, and the rewarding panoramic views.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in El Bosque?
A: Yes, many routes in El Bosque are loops. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5287361" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Mojón de la Víbora – Puerto de Montejaque loop from El Bosque&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40020056" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Palomas – El Boyar Pass loop from El Bosque&lt;/a&gt; are popular circular options.

Q: What are some notable viewpoints or landmarks along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer panoramic vistas from mountain passes. You can also explore natural attractions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4220058" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, or specific passes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/806461" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Palomas&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7401794" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to Puerto del Boyar from El Bosque&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in El Bosque?
A: While specific seasonal conditions can vary, the region's mountainous terrain means that spring and autumn generally offer more temperate weather for tackling the significant climbs. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring colder conditions to higher passes.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to refuel near the road cycling trails?
A: El Bosque itself offers amenities, and many routes pass through or near small towns and villages where you can find cafes and places to refuel. It's always advisable to check your specific route for available services along the way.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes in the area?
A: For experienced cyclists, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38166683" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de las Palomas – Pass of the Doves loop from El Bosque&lt;/a&gt;, with over 3,000 meters of elevation gain, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40019900" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Mojón de la Víbora – Espino Pass loop from El Bosque&lt;/a&gt;, are highly challenging options.

Q: Are there any natural monuments or points of interest to explore near the routes?
A: Beyond the mountain passes, you can find natural monuments such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3938203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Torreón Peak&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4722825" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Enchanted Forest&lt;/a&gt;, and the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4000431" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Green Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, all within the broader region.
tips: One of the most beautiful places in the province. Built between the 50s and 60s for the workers who built the press and their families.
Dam on the Majaceite River built in 1953. It offers views of the Los Hurones Reservoir. The dam is gravity type.
